22.1 Early Insights into D N A Replication

Information Transfer in the Cell
D N A  replication – D N A template-directed duplication of the genome prior to cell division
Transcription – D N A  template-directed biosynthesis of R N A
Reverse transcription – R N A  template-directed biosynthesis of D N A
Translation – m R N A template-directed biosynthesis of proteins

To Replicate D N A, the Strand Separation Occurs Simultaneously with D N A Synthesis (1 of 2)
D N A  strands are separated at regions called forks
Replication begins at one or more fixed sites called replication origins

To Replicate D N A, the Strand Separation Occurs Simultaneously with D N A Synthesis (2 of 2)
After replication each daughter cell receives one parental strand and one newly synthesized strand (semiconservative replication)

Bidirectional Replication in Bacteria
In bacteria, replication initiates from a fixed origin and proceeds bidirectionally
At the right, an autoradiogram of D  N  A showing two “Y” structures (red circles) – either or both could be a replication fork
Figure 22.1 Autoradiogram of a replicating E. coli chromosome after two generations of growth in
thymidine.
Figure 22.2 Demonstration of bidirectional replication by autoradiography.

Eukaryotic Replication of a Linear Chromosome (1 of 2)
Eukaryotic replication is bidirectional and proceeds from several fixed origins (indicated by the letter o at the top of the figure)
Figure 22.3 Bidirectional replication from several fixed origins (o) on a linear eukaryotic chromosome.

Eukaryotic Replication of a Linear Chromosome (2 of 2)
Forks advance until they meet another fork traveling in the opposite direction
Origins are programmed to initiate replication at fixed times in S phase
Figure 22.3 replication from several Bidirectional fixed origins (o) on a linear eukaryotic chromosome
22.2 D N A Polymerases: Enzymes Catalyzing Polynucleotide Chain Elongation

D N A Polymerase Reaction (1 of 3)
D N A polymerase catalyzes the nucleophilic attack by
at the primer terminus
upon the α-phosphate of the incoming d N T P that is based paired with the template
Figure 22.4 The D N A polymerase reaction.

D N A Polymerase Reaction (2 of 3)
This forms a new phosphodiester bond and releases pyrophosphate
Figure 22.4 The D N A polymerase reaction.

D N A Polymerase Reaction (3 of 3)
D   N   A polymerase requires D N A template, an RNA or D N A primer, and dNTPs
In addition to polymerase activity, DNA polymerase Ⅰ has
both
-exonuclease and
-
exonuclease activity (three active sites)
Figure 22.5 D N  A substrates that can be acted upon by purified D  N  A polymerase.

Structure of D N A Polymerase Roman numeral one.
(1 of 3)
The structure at right is of the large “Klenow fragment,” which comprises the
polymerase and
- exonuclease domain complexed with D N A
Figure 22.6 The Klenow
fragment of E. coli D N A
polymerase

Structure of D N A Polymerase Roman numeral one.
(2 of 3)
The small “Klenow fragment”
contains the
-exonuclease
domain
“Klenow fragments” are produced by limited proteolysis
Figure 22.6 The Klenow
fragment of E. coli D N A
polymerase

Structure of D N  A Polymerase  Roman numeral one.
(3 of 3)
There are several distinct D N A polymerases called types
but D N A polymerase
is the major replicative
polymerase in bacteria
D N A polymerase structure is often described as a hand, possessing a palm, fingers, and a thumb – in this way, the bound D N A can be completely surrounded

Structure of T7 Phage D N A Polymerase
Figure 22.7 Structure of T7 phage D  N A polymerase with template and primer bound.

The Two-Metal Mechanism of D N A Polymerase (1 of 2)
The complex at right shows ad d  C T P and D N A primer
with the
-O H of the primer
positioned for nucleophilic attack
Figure 22.8 The two-metal mechanism of the D N A polymerase reaction.

The Two-Metal Mechanism of D N A Polymerase (2 of 2)
ions coordinate the
substrates and stabilize the transition state
In this way the daughter strand
is synthesized in the
direction
Figure 22.8 The two-metal mechanism of the DNA polymerase reaction.
22.3 Other Proteins at the Replication Fork

Partial Genetic Map of Escherichia coli
The map shows genes involved in D N A replication or repair
Figure 22.9 Partial genetic map of E. coli.

Discontinuous D N A Synthesis at the Lagging Strand (1 of 2)
How are antiparallel D N A strands extended from the same
fork if polymerase extends in only one direction
?
How are new chains initiated if polymerase requires a preexisting terminus?
Okazaki’s model required discontinuous synthesis of the lagging strand, copying it in short fragments (Okazaki fragments) which would be attached by D N A ligase
Discontinuous D N A Synthesis at the Lagging Strand (2 of 2)

Model of D N A Replication Involving Discontinuous Synthesis on the Lagging Strand
Figure 22.12 A model for D   N  A replication involving discontinuous synthesis on the lagging strand.

D N A Ligase Reaction
Figure 22.13 The reaction catalyzed by D   N  A ligase.

Nick Translation in the Removal of R N A Primers
Primase synthesizes R N A primers for initiation of replication
The R N A primer must be removed and replaced with deoxynucleotides
This is accomplished by the exonuclease and polymerase activities of D N A polymerase Ⅰ
polymerase activities of D N A polymerase Ⅰ
Figure 22.14 Nick translation in the removal of RNA primers by coordinated
action of the
exonuclease and

Schematic View of the Replication Fork
Figure 22.15 Schematic view of a replication fork.

Substructure of the Escherichia coli D N A Polymerase III (1 of 4)
D N A polymerase III exists as a holoenzyme
The core polymerase is composed of the α, ε, and θ subunits
Figure 22.16 Subunit structure of the E. coli D N A polymerase III holoenzyme.

Substructure of the Escherichia coli D N A Polymerase III (2 of 4)
The β subunit is a sliding clamp that helps to keep the D N A polymerase bound to the template during many rounds of nucleotide addition
Figure 22.16 Subunit structure of the E. coli D N A polymerase III holoenzyme.

Substructure of the Escherichia coli D N A Polymerase III (3 of 4)
The τ proteins dimerize the two D N A polymerase III cores
Figure 22.16 Subunit structure of the E. coli D N A polymerase III holoenzyme.

Substructure of the Escherichia coli D N A Polymerase III (4 of 4)
The remainder of the proteins shown in this image form the clamp loading complex, responsible for placing the “doughnut”-shaped β subunit around the D N A template
Figure 22.16 Subunit structure of the E. coli D N A polymerase III holoenzyme.

Structures of the Sliding Clamp from Three Organisms
Figure 22.17 Structure of the sliding clamp.
Although composed of different numbers of subunits, the overall structures are very similar

Scheme of Action of the Escherichia coli Clamp Loader
Exchange of A D P for A T P increases affinity of the loader for the clamp
Upon binding, the loader opens the clamp
Binding of the template-primer D N A leads to A T P hydrolysis, closure of the clamp and release of the loader
Figure 22.18 Scheme for action of the E. coli clamp loader (a) and structures of three clamp-loading complexes (b).

Model for the Helicase-Mediated D N A Unwinding (1 of 2)
Figure 22.19 A model for helicase action.

Model for the Helicase-Mediated D N A Unwinding (2 of 2)
Helicase catalyzes the A T P-dependent unwinding of the double-stranded D N A to form single-stranded D N A
The unwinding of D N A by helicase would cause the double stranded D N A to become overwound, if there were no mechanism to relieve the torsional stress (accomplished by topoisomerase)

Topoisomerase Relieves Torsional Stress in D N A (1 of 2)
Figure 22.21 Action of a type I topoisomerase.

Topoisomerase Relieves Torsional Stress in D N A (2 of 2)
Topoisomerase I relieves this stress by nicking, one strand of D N A by forming a covalent phosphodiester bond to the
-phosphate
The
end is then free to rotate to relieve the strain; the
torsionally relaxed D N A is religated and unwinding by helicase can continue
Type I topoisomerases change the linking number by

Type II Topoisomerases, Like D N A Gyrase, Catalyze a Double Strand Break
topoisomerases change the linking number by
Figure 22.22 Action of a type II topoisomerase.

Structure of Topoisomerase I
Topoisomerase
completely wraps around the D N A
Topoisomerase inhibitors have been developed to treat cancer
Figure 22.23 Crystal structure
of human topoisomerase
in complex with a 22-bp D N A duplex.

Topology Changes Catalyzed by Type II Topoisomerases
Topological inter-conversions in circular D N A catalyzed by type II topoisomerases:
Knotting
Unknotting
Catenation (linking)
Decatenation (unlinking)
Figure 22.24 The types of topological interconversions catalyzed by type II topoisomerases.

Topoisomerase Action in Termination of Replication
Topoisomerase Ⅳ allows the duplicated circular D N A to decatenate through one of two mechanisms shown
Figure 22.25 Topoisomerase action in termination of replication.

Overview of the Escherichia coli Replisome
Figure 22.26 The E. coli replisome.
22.4 Eukaryotic D N A Replication

Model of the Yeast Replisome
Eukaryotic D N A polymerase ε does not require a sliding clamp due to the addition of the P domain
Figure 22.27 Model of the yeast replisome, based on high-resolution electron microscopy.
Proteins Involved in Replication Processes in Prokaryotes, Viruses, and Eukaryotes

Model for Chromatin Replication
Figure 22.28 Model for chromatin replication.
22.5 Initiation of D N A Replication

Initiation of Replication in Escherichia coli  (1 of 2)
Initiation of replication occurs
at the origin sequence,
which contains four 9 bp repeats
Figure 22.29 A model for the initiation of E. coli D N  A
replication at

Initiation of Replication in Escherichia coli  (2 of 2)
The three 13 bp repeats above the origin are A T rich and facilitate D N A bending
Figure 22.29 A model for the initiation of E. coli DNA
replication at

Preparing a Fission Yeast Replication Origin for Initiation
Replication in eukaryotes requires that replication origins become active at different times
Figure 22.30 Preparing a fission yeast replication origin for initiation.
22.6 Replication of Linear Genomes

The Problem of Completing the
-end in
Copying a Linear D N A Molecule
Excision of the R N A primer would leave a gap that cannot be filled
because there is no
primer
terminus to extend
Viruses have evolved three strategies to overcome this problem
Eukaryotes solve this problem via the use of telomeres
Figure 22.31 The problem of
completing the
end in
copying a linear D N A molecule.

Viruses Use Three Strategies to Complete the 5′-end (1 of 2)
Terminal redundancy and recombination (at right)
Covalently linking two strands at the end of the linear genome, so the final lagging strand can be replaced by D N A (as in a circular complex)
Figure 22.32 Strategies for replicating linear genomes.

Viruses Use Three Strategies to Complete the 5′-end (2 of 2)
Use of a d C M P-linked terminal protein (at right)
Figure 22.32 Strategies for replicating linear genomes.

Extension of Telomeric D N A by Telomerase  (1 of 2)
Telomeres are special tandemly repeated sequences located at the ends of each chromosome
Typically, one strand is G-rich and the other is C-rich
These repeated short segments of D N A prevent the loss of coding sequences after repeated rounds of replication

Extension of Telomeric D N A by Telomerase  (2 of 2)
Figure 22.33 Extension of telomeric D N A by human telomerase.
22.7 Fidelity of D N A Replication

D N A Proofreading Activity of D N A Polymerase (1 of 2)
D N A proofreading of D N A polymerase excises mismatched nucleotides to maintain fidelity in D N A replication
Accuracy is determined at the nucleotide insertion step and
the
exonucleolytic proofreading step
Mismatches increase
reducing the speed of
extension; this delay in extension allows unwinding into the exonuclease site

D N A Proofreading Activity of D N A Polymerase (2 of 2)
Figure 22.34 by a
-exonuclease site distant from the
polymerase site. Kinetic basis for preferential excision of mismatched nucleotides

D N A Polymerase Insertion Specificity (1 of 2)
Specificity during nucleotide insertion occurs at steps 2 and 3

D N A Polymerase Insertion Specificity (2 of 2)
Studies using d T T P analogs demonstrated that specificity during incorporation is due to both hydrogen-bonding and shape recognition

Huntington’s Disease Arises from D N A Polymerase Slippage Errors (1 of 2)
Huntington’s disease is an autosomal dominant neurological disease
The brain protein huntingtin contains a stretch of glutamine
residues, encoded by
-C A G-
Normal individuals have between 6 and 31 of these C A G repeats
Individuals with the disease have as many as 80

Huntington’s Disease Arises from D N A Polymerase Slippage Errors (2 of 2)
The number of repeats is thought to increase, if the D N A product slips out forming a loop during replication

D N T P Pool Imbalance Can Lead to Replication Errors
Figure 22.35 How d N T P pool imbalance can lead to replication errors
22.8 R N A Viruses: The Replication of RNA Genomes

R N A-Dependent R N A Replicases
Most R  N  A viruses have a single molecule of single-stranded
R  N  A that is the sense or
strand
Replicase uses the
strand to produce a
strand
which serves as a template for the synthesis of more
strands that are packaged into progeny virions
Rapid mutation rates are seen in R N A viruses as they do not possess proofreading mechanisms

Retroviruses Use Reverse Transcriptase to Produce D N A from an R N A Template
Figure 22.36 Simplified view of the retrovirus life cycle

Chapter 22 Summary (1 of 2)
D N A replication initiates from fixed replication origins and proceeds semiconservatively and bidirectionally
During D N A replication, D N A polymerases catalyze the template-directed incorporation of deoxyribo-nucleotides
moving in a
-to-
direction
Other proteins involved in various steps of D N A replication are D N A ligase, single-strand D N A-binding protein, primase, sliding clamp, clamp loaders, helicase, and topoisomerases

Chapter 22 Summary (2 of 2)
Initiation of D N A replication begins with duplex D N A unwinding to expose single-stranded templates
Special mechanisms such as telomerase activity are required to complete the replication process of both D N A strands
Several processes contribute to the high accuracy of D N A
replication including
-exonucleolytic proofreading,
polymerase insertion specificity, and maintenance of balanced D N A precursor pools
